title: Acrostichum pennula
intro: Acrostichum pennula, also known as the mangrove fern, is a species of epiphytic fern native to tropical and subtropical regions. This plant has been traditionally used in medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in treating various conditions such as wounds, skin infections, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Acrostichum pennula include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include decoctions, infusions, and ointments, which are used to treat a range of health issues in traditional medicine.
taxonomy: Acrostichum pennula is a species of fern in the family Dryopteridaceae, commonly known as the wood ferns or scaly male ferns. The family Dryopteridaceae is a group of vascular plants that are typically found in terrestrial habitats. Acrostichum pennula is native to tropical and subtropical regions around the world, including parts of Asia, Africa, and the Americas. This species of fern is known for its unique characteristics and growth habits, which set it apart from other members of the Dryopteridaceae family. The classification of Acrostichum pennula highlights its complex relationships within the plant kingdom.
